did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Vision Transformers (ViT) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs like DenseNet201), combined with transfer learning and bagging-ensemble learning, to classify seven types of skin lesions including monkeypox and melanoma from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a quantitative classification tool for skin lesions using Vision Transformers and CNNs. It details specific methods (transfer learning, bagging-ensemble learning), datasets (PAD-UFES-20, MSLD), and reports new empirical results (81.91% Accuracy, etc.) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Vision transformer and CNN-based skin lesion analysis: classification of monkeypox
brief_summary: This study developed a quantitative and objective classification tool to assist in the rapid diagnosis of monkeypox and other skin lesions, including melanoma. The researchers combined the PAD-UFES-20 and MSLD datasets to create a seven-class skin lesion classification task. They employed transfer learning to fine-tune a Vision Transformer pre-trained on ImageNet-21k and several Convolutional Neural Networks, such as DenseNet201. The best-performing individual models were then integrated using bagging-ensemble learning to generate final predictions. The proposed ensemble system achieved an accuracy of 81.91%, with precision, recall, and F-score values of 87.16%, 74.12%, and 78.16%, respectively. These results demonstrate that the system competes with or outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms on the same dataset, offering a robust solution for dermatological decision-making.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the combined PAD-UFES-20 and MSLD dataset used for classification. The study focuses on classifying monkeypox lesions alongside other skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Seven-class skin lesion categories (including monkeypox, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify seven skin lesions' and reports metrics such as Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F-score, and Jaccard index for a multi-class classification task. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories from dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public skin lesion dataset originally containing six cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions) and nevi. It was combined with the MSLD dataset for this study.

use.training: Combined with MSLD to form the 7-class dataset for training Vision Transformer and CNN models via transfer learning.
use.validation: Part of the combined dataset used for validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing the 6-class classification system and as part of the 7-class combined dataset for testing the ensemble system.
use.other: Used for fair comparison with literature studies that utilized only the 6-class PAD-UFES-20 data.

datasets_used.name: MSLD (Monkeypox Skin Lesion Dataset)
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing human monkeypox skin lesion images. It was combined with PAD-UFES-20 to create a 7-class skin lesion classification task.

use.training: Combined with PAD-UFES-20 to form the 7-class dataset for training Vision Transformer and CNN models.
use.validation: Part of the combined dataset used for validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing the 7-class classification system.

datasets_used.name: Combined PAD-UFES-20 + MSLD Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom combined dataset created by the authors for this study, containing 7 types of skin lesions (6 from PAD-UFES-20 and Monkeypox from MSLD).

use.training: Primary dataset for training the Vision Transformer and DenseNet201 models using transfer learning.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing the final bagging-ensemble model and individual models.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet-21k
datasets_used.description: A large-scale dataset of 14 million labeled images used for pre-training the Vision Transformer model before fine-tuning on the skin lesion data.

use.training: Pre-training source for the Vision Transformer model weights.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To combine the predictions of the best-performing individual models (Vision Transformer and DenseNet201) to improve classification accuracy and robustness.
To leverage pre-trained weights (on ImageNet-21k) for fine-tuning models on the skin lesion dataset, compared against training from scratch to demonstrate performance benefits with limited data.
To provide balanced data and cope with the small amount of available data by applying rotation, reflection, translation, and scale to the training set.
To visualize and analyze the classification performance, identifying specific errors (e.g., worst performance for SCC lesions) and comparing model behaviors on specific image types.
To compare the proposed system's metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F-score, Jaccard) against state-of-the-art literature results on the same datasets (PAD-UFES-20 and combined PAD-UFES-20+MSLD).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F-score, Jaccard) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

Combined PAD-UFES-20 + MSLD (7-class).notes: The ensemble model outperformed individual models. The worst performance was observed for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) lesions. The Vision Transformer model trained from scratch achieved 60.49% accuracy compared to 80.66% with transfer learning.

PAD-UFES-20 (6-class).notes: The Vision Transformer model trained on the 6-class PAD-UFES-20 dataset outperformed literature studies [52-55] in Accuracy, Precision, and Fscore. In terms of Recall, it was second behind study [52] and better than others.

Literature Comparisons (PAD-UFES-20 6-cl.notes: Comparative results against previous studies using the same 6-class PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

Literature Comparisons (PAD-UFES-20 + Mo.notes: The proposed ensemble system outperformed the previous study [30] which used the same 7-class dataset in terms of all indices.

architecture_used.main_model: Vision Transformer (ViT) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically DenseNet201, combined via Bagging Ensemble Learning.
architecture_used.pretraining: Vision Transformer pre-trained on ImageNet-21k (14 million labeled images). CNNs (VGG16, SqueezeNet, Inception_v3, ResNet50, MobileNet_v3, DenseNet201) pre-trained on ImageNet.
architecture_used.input_size: Vision Transformer: Input images divided into fixed-size 16x16 patches. CNNs: VGG16 (224x224), SqueezeNet (227x227), Inception_v3 (299x299), ResNet50 (224x224), MobileNet_v3 (224x224), DenseNet201 (224x224).

vision_transformer.encoder: Transformer encoder with alternate multi-headed self-attention layers and multi-layer perceptron blocks
vision_transformer.modification: Pre-trained prediction label removed; zero-initialized D x N feedforward layer attached (N = number of downstream classes, D = hidden size)

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Bagging Ensemble Learning (Bootstrap Aggregating) combining Vision Transformer and DenseNet201

training_details.strategy: Transfer Learning (fine-tuning) compared against Training from Scratch (random weight initialization)
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, reflection, translation, and scale applied to training set
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (standard classification loss implied)

classification_or_output_head.vision_transformer: Zero-initialized D x N feedforward layer (linear layer) attached to pre-trained ViT
classification_or_output_head.cnn_models: Fully connected layers modified for classification (specific layer details not reported)
classification_or_output_head.ensemble: Bagging strategy combining predictions from Vision Transformer and DenseNet201

preprocessing_techniques: Image patching: Input images divided into fixed-size 16x16 patches for the Vision Transformer model.
Linear embedding: Patches linearly embedded to form tokens.
Classification token addition: A learnable classification specifier (token) added to the beginning of the sequence.
Position embedding: Absolute position embeddings added to the sequence.
Data augmentation: Rotation, reflection, translation, and scale applied to the training set to cope with small data size and provide balanced data.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, reflection, translation, and scale were applied to the training set to provide balanced data and cope with the small amount of available data.

conclusions: The proposed automated system successfully classifies seven types of skin lesions, including monkeypox, by combining the PAD-UFES-20 and MSLD datasets.

Transfer learning is effective for skin lesion classification with limited data; the Vision Transformer model fine-tuned on ImageNet-21k achieved 80.66% accuracy, significantly outperforming the same model trained from scratch (60.49%).
Among individual models tested, the Vision Transformer (transfer learning) achieved the highest accuracy (80.66%), followed by DenseNet201 (75.88%).

The bagging-ensemble learning approach, combining the Vision Transformer and DenseNet201 models, improved classification performance, achieving 81.91% accuracy, 87.16% precision, 74.12% recall, and 78.16% F-score.
The ensemble system outperformed state-of-the-art algorithms and previous literature studies on the same combined 7-class dataset in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F-score.
When evaluated on the 6-class PAD-UFES-20 dataset, the Vision Transformer model outperformed literature studies in accuracy, precision, and F-score, and achieved comparable recall results.
The Vision Transformer model's attention mechanism allows it to effectively capture long-range features, making it particularly successful when lesions cover a large portion of the image.
DenseNet201 demonstrated superior performance in classifying lesions concentrated in small, specific regions of the image, highlighting the complementary strengths of CNNs and Transformers.

The system's performance was lowest for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) lesions, which were associated with low-quality images taken with mobile phone cameras, suggesting that image quality impacts diagnostic success.
The developed system offers a quantitative, objective, and rapid tool to assist dermatologists and scientists in decision-making, particularly for urgent diagnoses like monkeypox.
important_remarks: The study combines the PAD-UFES-20 and MSLD datasets to create a 7-class classification task, but does not report the specific number of images per class or total dataset size, limiting reproducibility and assessment of class balance.

The dataset is small, necessitating transfer learning; the paper explicitly notes that Vision Transformers do not generalize well on insufficient data without pre-training, highlighting a dependency on large external datasets (ImageNet-21k).

Performance was notably weak for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) lesions, which the authors attribute to low-quality images taken with mobile phone cameras, indicating a potential bias towards image quality and modality.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons against state-of-the-art methods, relying solely on descriptive metrics.

The ensemble model (Bagging) combines only the Vision Transformer and DenseNet201; it is unclear if other high-performing individual models were excluded from the ensemble, potentially limiting the full potential of ensemble learning.

The system was evaluated on a combined dataset of public sources (PAD-UFES-20 and MSLD) rather than a single, uniformly collected clinical cohort, which may introduce domain shift and heterogeneity issues not addressed.

No external validation on an independent, unseen dataset was performed; results are based on an 80/20 split of the combined public datasets, raising concerns about overfitting to these specific sources.

The paper claims the system is 'cheap' and suitable for rapid diagnosis but does not report inference time, computational cost, or hardware requirements, which are critical for deployment feasibility.
